text: The surgeon releases adhesions on the anterior aspect and lateral pelvic wall using scissors and monopolar current. Adhesions between the omentum and vaginal wall are also released. Traction is applied to the rectum towards the left side, while dissection occurs on both the posterior and anterior aspects. The plane between the vaginal vault and rectum is opened, with circumferential dissection of the anterior plane, joining it with the posteriorly dissected plane. The vaginal vault is visibly separated from the rectum.

The surgeon uses a hook and monopolar current to dissect the loose areolar tissue in the posterior plane, providing mobility of the rectum by approximately four to five centimeters anteriorly. Adequate traction on the rectum aids in identifying the correct dissection plane.

The surgeon marks the anatomical plane and identifies loose areolar tissue, entering the correct dissection plane from right to left, top to bottom. Dissection continues on the left side, ensuring the left ureter is safeguarded. The posterior aspect is developed, and once adequate dissection is achieved, the planes on the left side, posteriorly and anteriorly, are joined.

The surgeon joins the anterior and posterior planes, opening up the pre-sacral space. A polycrippling mesh, measuring ten centimeters, is fixed to the sacral promontory using absorbable tackles. The left aspect plane is closed, followed by the closure of the right side peritoneal port, and the mesh is completely retroperitonealized.
The surgeon uses non-absorbable sutures to secure the mesh, which is now completely retroperitonealized, aiding in pulling up the rectum during the laparoscopic rectopexy procedure.
